summary: Steve Yegge humorously addresses a Hacker News misunderstanding where users thought he quit Google during an OSCON talk, when he actually just quit a specific project he wasn't passionate about. He uses this as a springboard to enthusiastically recommend working at Google while acknowledging their interview process is flawed. He reveals he's been working for 3.5 years on a project to turn source code into something like Wikipedia, and announces his intention to eventually pivot toward machine learning and cancer research.
key_points: Hacker News misinterpreted his OSCON talk, thinking he quit Google when he actually quit a project within Google
Google's interview process 'sucks at letting in all the qualified people' because interviewers want candidates exactly like themselves
He violated his own advice from his talk by working on something he wasn't passionate about, so he course-corrected
He's been working on a project to transform source code from plain text into 'Wikipedia' for 3.5 years
He's studying math and statistics to eventually work on machine learning problems, particularly cancer research
money_quotes: Google's interview process -- what's the word I'm looking for here -- ah yes, their process sucks at letting in all the qualified people.

The real problem is the classic interview process, which everyone uses and which Google hasn't innovated on, not really. It's like deciding whether to marry someone after four one-hour dates that all happen on the same day in a little room that looks kind of like a doctor's office except that the examining table is on the wall.
I'm getting old and I only have so many 'big projects' left that I can actually participate in.
It is a project that aims to turn source code -- ALL source code -- from plain text into Wikipedia.
It's a complete cop-out for me to take the easy path and work on a project that my company is excited about but I am not.
I've demonstrated to myself quite a few times that if I do anything daily for a few years I can get pretty good at it.
key_insight: Life is too short to work on projects you're not passionate about, even if your company is excited about them.
